title: Input & Output
description: Reading user input, printing output, string formatting techniques (f-strings, .format(), %-formatting), and the print function's advanced options.

sections.title: Basic Output (print)
sections.description: Print outputs values. You can customize separators, end characters, and redirect print outputs directly to files.
sections.codeBlock:
# Simple console print
print("Hello, World!")

# Printing multiple values (space-separated by default)
print("The answer is", 42)

# Custom separator and custom end character
print("a", "b", "c", sep=", ", end="!\n") # a, b, c!

# Redirect output to file
with open("log.txt", "w") as f:
    print("[ERROR] something broke", file=f)

sections.title: User Input (input)
sections.description: The input function halts execution and reads a line from the user. It always returns a string, which can be explicitly type-cast.
sections.codeBlock:
# Read string input
name = input("Enter your name: ")

# Read numeric input and cast it
age = int(input("Enter your age: "))

sections.title: Formatted Output (f-strings)
sections.description: F-strings provide a readable and efficient way to embed expressions inside string literals using curly braces.
sections.codeBlock:
name = "Alice"
age = 30

# Basic f-string interpolation
print(f"{name} is {age} years old")

# Evaluating expressions at runtime
print(f"{2 * 3 + 1}") # "7"

sections.title: Format Specifiers
sections.description: F-strings support a robust mini-language for aligning text, specifying numeric precision, showing binary conversions, and formatting large numbers.
sections.codeBlock:
pi = 3.14159
large_num = 1000000
name = "Alice"

# Floating-point precision
print(f"{pi:.2f}") # "3.14"

# Thousands separator
print(f"{large_num:,}") # "1,000,000"

# Integer to binary formatting
print(f"{42:08b}") # "00101010"

# Alignment and Padding
print(f"{name:>10}") # "     Alice" (right align)
print(f"{name:<10}") # "Alice     " (left align)
print(f"{name:^10}") # "  Alice   " (center align)

# Debug representation (repr)
print(f"{name!r}") # "'Alice'"

sections.title: Legacy Formatting (.format & %)
sections.description: Older versions of Python use the .format() string method and C-style % modulo operator. Avoid using these in new codebases.
sections.codeBlock:
name = "Alice"
age = 30
pi = 3.14159

# positional and keyword .format() formatting
fmt1 = "{} is {} years old".format(name, age)
print(fmt1)          # Alice is 30 years old
fmt2 = "{n} is {a} years old".format(n=name, a=age)
print(fmt2)          # Alice is 30 years old

# C-style modulo formatting
fmt_legacy = "%s is %d years old" % (name, age)
print(fmt_legacy)    # Alice is 30 years old
fmt_float = "pi = %.2f" % pi
print(fmt_float)     # pi = 3.14

details:
The print() function is Python's primary output mechanism. By default it appends a newline and separates arguments with a space, but both are customizable via the sep and end keyword arguments. The file argument redirects output to any object with a .write() method — commonly a file handle, but also sys.stdout, sys.stderr, or io.StringIO buffers.

The input() function reads a line from standard input, always returning a string. Convert to the desired type explicitly (int(), float()) — a non-numeric input raises ValueError, so wrap in try/except for robust programs. There is no built-in input_int() in Python; the pattern int(input(...)) is idiomatic.

F-strings (PEP 498) are the modern and preferred string formatting approach. They evaluate arbitrary Python expressions at runtime, support format specifiers (precision, alignment, type coercion, grouping), and are generally faster than both .format() and %-formatting. Inside f-strings, use ! to apply repr(), str(), or ascii() conversion.

The older str.format() method (Python 2.6+) supports positional and keyword arguments with the same format specifier mini-language. The % operator (C printf-style) is the oldest approach, still found in legacy codebases but avoided in new code. The string module's Formatter class provides programmatic access to the formatting logic.

For the full formatting mini-language specification, see Format Specification Mini-Language and the print() documentation.
